skill.name: azure-storage-blob-py
skill.description: Guidance for using the Azure Blob Storage SDK for Python to authenticate, manage containers, transfer blobs, tune performance, and handle metadata.
skill.summary: Use Azure Blob Storage from Python with SDK examples for common container and blob workflows.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: The static analyzer flagged markdown backticks, Azure URLs, one local file write example, and a weak-crypto signal. Review found no malicious instructions, no prompt injection, no executable skill scripts, and no evidence of weak cryptography. The remaining concerns are expected for an Azure Blob Storage guide because users may access cloud storage and local files when they run the examples.

low_findings.title: Expected Azure Network Access Examples
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URLs are Azure Blob Storage endpoint examples using placeholder account names. This is expected for the skill purpose and does not show exfiltration or hidden destinations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs use the standard Azure Blob Storage domain with placeholder account values. The surrounding context is SDK documentation for connecting to Azure storage.

low_findings.title: Expected Local File Read and Write Examples
low_findings.description: The file operations demonstrate uploading a local file and saving a downloaded blob. These operations are user-directed examples and do not write hidden paths or modify system files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file paths are simple local examples and the operations are part of blob upload and download workflows. No persistence, stealth, or sensitive path targeting is present.

low_findings.title: Static Backtick and Weak Crypto Findings Dismissed
low_findings.description: The Ruby backtick findings are markdown fences or inline code examples, not executable Ruby or shell code. The weak cryptographic algorithm alert at the front matter has no supporting evidence in the file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced lines are front matter, fenced code markers, or markdown inline code. I found no Ruby execution syntax and no MD5, SHA1, DES, RC4, or similar weak cryptographic use.

content.user_title: Build Azure Blob Workflows in Python
content.value_statement: Python developers often need clear Azure Blob Storage examples without searching SDK docs. This skill gives focused patterns for authentication, containers, uploads, downloads, listings, metadata, SAS tokens, and async clients.
actual_capabilities: Shows how to install azure-storage-blob and azure-identity.
Provides examples for creating containers and uploading blobs.
Provides examples for downloading blobs to files, memory, or buffers.
Covers listing blobs, walking virtual directories, and deleting blobs.
Includes examples for SAS tokens, metadata, performance tuning, and async clients.
limitations: It is a documentation skill, not an executable Azure client.
It does not provision Azure accounts, containers, identities, or roles.
It uses placeholder account names and paths that users must replace.
It does not validate permissions, network access, or storage policies.

use_cases.title: Implement Blob Uploads
use_cases.description: Add Python code that uploads local files, byte content, or streams to an Azure Blob container.

use_cases.title: Review Storage Access Patterns
use_cases.description: Check whether a Python service uses recommended Azure identity and blob client patterns.

use_cases.title: Optimize Large Transfers
use_cases.description: Tune concurrency and chunk settings for large blob uploads and downloads.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Basic Client
prompt_templates.prompt: Show me how to connect to Azure Blob Storage in Python using DefaultAzureCredential and BlobServiceClient.

prompt_templates.title: Upload and Download Files
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me write Python code to upload a local file to Azure Blob Storage and download it back to a local path.

prompt_templates.title: List and Manage Blobs
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a Python workflow that lists blobs by prefix, reads blob properties, updates metadata, and deletes a selected blob.

prompt_templates.title: Design an Async Transfer Flow
prompt_templates.prompt: Design an async Python Azure Blob Storage workflow for high-volume uploads with concurrency settings and clear resource cleanup.

output_examples.input: I need to upload a report file to Azure Blob Storage from Python.
output_examples.output: A concise upload plan using DefaultAzureCredential, BlobServiceClient, a container name, a blob name, and an explicit overwrite setting.

output_examples.input: I need to list log blobs under a folder-like prefix.
output_examples.output: A container listing approach that uses a prefix filter and explains how virtual directory walking differs from flat listing.

output_examples.input: I need temporary read access to one blob.
output_examples.output: A SAS token approach with read-only permission, a short expiry time, and guidance to protect account keys.

best_practices: Use DefaultAzureCredential when possible instead of embedding connection strings.
Set explicit overwrite behavior for uploads so replacement behavior is clear.
Use streaming, readinto, and concurrency settings for large transfer workloads.
anti_patterns: Do not hardcode real account keys, connection strings, or SAS tokens in source files.
Do not use broad SAS permissions when a read-only or short-lived token is enough.
Do not load large blobs fully into memory when a streaming approach is available.

faq.question: Does this skill run Azure commands for me?
faq.answer: No. It provides Python SDK guidance and examples that users can adapt in their own environment.

faq.question: Which authentication method does it recommend?
faq.answer: It recommends DefaultAzureCredential for common development and production identity flows.

faq.question: Can it help with SAS tokens?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes a read-only blob SAS example with a short expiration period.

faq.question: Does it cover async Azure Blob clients?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes async client examples for upload and download workflows.

faq.question: Can it create Azure resources automatically?
faq.answer: No. It shows SDK usage but does not provision accounts, roles, or infrastructure.

faq.question: Is it safe to publish?
faq.answer: Yes. The audit found documentation examples for expected storage operations and no malicious behavior.
